literal: that which is heard; from √śru, 'to hear'
invariant_definition: *Śruti* is not merely a sacred book. It is the heard Vedic corpus taken, in Mīmāṃsā and most Vedānta, as *apauruṣeya* speech whose authority is intrinsic and whose sentences function as *śabda-pramāṇa* where perception and inference do not reach. The term therefore sits inside a larger apparatus: the distinction between *vidhi*, *mantra*, and *arthavāda*; the split between *karma-kāṇḍa* and *jñāna-kāṇḍa*; the need to establish a passage's settled purport (*tātparya*) by *samanvaya* rather than by isolated quotation; and the claim that Vedic language discloses real obligations and real metaphysical structure. In practice a thinker citing *śruti* is normally also presupposing a hermeneutic method for reconciling apparently divergent passages. The major disagreements concern what the corpus is finally about: injunction and *dharma* for Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, *brahman* for Vedānta, qualified *brahman* for Viśiṣṭādvaita, Viṣṇu for Tattva-vāda (the school often labeled Dvaita), and Kṛṣṇa as *svayaṃ-bhagavān* for Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇavism. Thus *śruti* names both a canon and a theory of revelatory language.

per_school.definition: Śruti alone is the operative *pramāṇa* for *ātma-jñāna* and *brahma-jñāna*, since the Veda alone is held to disclose the knowledge whose fruit is liberation [[UpS I.17.8](cite://sankara/upadesa-sahasri/I.17.8)] [[MBs 1.2](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.2)]. Its *tātparya* is fixed by disciplined differentiation of *vācya* and *lakṣya*: creation-śrutis determine the referent of *tat*, waking-dream-sleep śrutis determine the referent of *tvam*, and the negation of plurality marks the siddhānta of *brahmātmaikya* [[Upodghāta 9](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/9)] [[Upodghāta 10](cite://madhusudana/siddhanta-bindu/10)] [[BS 1.1 (plurality as māyā-bound)](cite://mandana/brahma-siddhi/1.1.1--opening-prose-on-ekatva-and-m-y)].

Śruti is a single Vedāntic body whose head-texts establish meditation, worship, and surrender toward the supreme Person as the settled import of revelation [[VS §3](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§3)]. The same corpus discloses Nārāyaṇa as the *antaryāmin* of all *cit* and *acit* through *sāmānādhikaraṇya*, while non-duality-statements exclude any second equal or superior to him rather than the real order of the whole [[VS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)] [[Svs 31-35](cite://yamuna/siddhi-trayam/31-35)].

Śruti is a bheda-bearing *pramāṇa* whose *sāmarthya* makes the distinction of *jīva* and Īśvara firm, while passages of apparent non-difference are read through *sādṛśya*, *prādhānya*, and transferred designation rather than *svarūpābheda* [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)] [[TD 1.3](cite://raghavendra/tantra-dipika/1.3)]. The same śruti-body teaches Viṣṇu's *sarvottamatva*, declaring his auspicious qualities and his abiding superiority to the *jīva* even where likeness-language is admitted [[Verse 14](cite://madhva/upadhi-khandana/14)] [[Verse 18](cite://madhva/upadhi-khandana/18)].

The scriptural corpus is received as containing both *bheda-śāstras* and *abheda-śāstras*, and their concord is secured through Bhagavān's *svābhāvika acintya-śakti* rather than by collapsing either register [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42)] [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. Once scripture has fixed the distinction between the *māyā-āśraya* Lord and the *māyā-mohita* jīva, it establishes Bhagavān-bhajana as *abhidheya* and *parama-prema* for Bhagavān as *prayojana* [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §44](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§44)] [[Paramātma-Sandarbha §45](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§45)].

Śruti is hermeneutically one because the aggregate of revelation reaches *avirodha* when referred to Śrī Kṛṣṇa [[Maṅgalācaraṇa 1](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/1)]. Its settled teaching on the *jīva-brahman* relation is *aṃśatva*: the jīva is neither *atyantabhinna* nor *atyantābhinna*, but a dependent *śakti* and therefore *asvatantra* [[BS 2.3.42](cite://srinivasa/vedanta-kaustubha/2.3.42)] [[VPS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42)].

Śruti is decisive where ordinary ontology does not reach, because Brahman and the jīva's *aṃśatva* are *vedaika-samadhigamya* and are not settled from common experience [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)]. The same Vedic disclosure teaches an *avikṛta-pariṇāma* in which Brahman truly appears in effect-form while remaining unmodified, so scriptural causality is both real and non-diminishing [[BSB 1.4.26](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/1.4.26)] [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)].

For Sāṅkhya, *āptaśruti* or *āptāgama* is one member of the threefold *pramāṇa*-scheme, alongside perception and inference, and its authority is that of reliable testimony rather than of a sui generis revelatory organ [[SK 5](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/5)]. Its proper field is the *parokṣa* and *atīndriya*: what is not secured by perception may still be established from the trustworthy tradition of the competent speaker [[SK 6](cite://isvarakrsna/sankhya-karika/6)].

Yoga accepts *āgama* as a *pramāṇa* when a trustworthy knower communicates a seen or inferred object for another's cognition; if the speaker lacks such warrant, the putative testimony simply 'floats' (*plavate*) [[YBh 1.7](cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.7)]. Yet scriptural and inferential cognition remain *sāmānyaviṣaya*; the *viśeṣa* needed for yogic knowledge of subtle, remote, or inward realities is grasped only by *samādhi-prajñā*, whose object is other than that of *śruta* and *anumāna* [[YBh 1.49](cite://vyasa-bhasya/yoga-sutra-bhasya/1.49)].

In the Bhikṣu synthesis, śruti fixes the cosmological use of *māyā* by prescribing that the term denotes *prakṛti*, already known from other śruti and smṛti, under the lordship of Maheśvara [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)]. This is a realist hermeneutic of causal disclosure rather than of merely illusory appearance, and the appeal to scripture is what secures that reading [[VĀB 1.1.5](cite://vijnanabhiksu/vijnanamrta-bhasya/1.1.5)].

For Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika, the analogue of *śruti* is *śabda-pramāṇa* as *āptopadeśa*: authoritative testimony is the utterance of a competent speaker who has directly apprehended the relevant object and intends to communicate it truly [[NS 1.1.7](cite://aksapada/nyaya-sutra/1.1.7)] [[NBh on NS 1.1.7](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/1.1.7)]. Vedic injunctions concerning supersensible ends such as *svarga* are paradigmatic precisely where neither perception nor *liṅga*-based inference operates, so scripture functions there as an independent route to cognition [[NBh p.92](cite://vatsyayana/nyaya-sutra-bhasya/p92-agnihotra-juhuyat-svargakamah)].
primary_loci: Nyāya-Bhāṣya, p.92 example on `agnihotra juhuyāt svargakāmaḥ`

per_school.definition: In Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, *śruti* is first and foremost *codanā*: the Vedic injunction whose object is *dharma* and whose authority is irreducible to perception or inference [[MS 1.1.2](cite://jaimini/mimamsa-sutra/1.1.2)] [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.2](cite://shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.2)]. Because *dharma* is supersensible, the Veda alone discloses it; Śabara accordingly treats Vedic utterance as non-errant in its own sphere and refuses to assimilate it to fallible human speech [[ŚBh on MS 1.1.2](cite://shabara/shabara-bhasya/1.1.2)].

For Yogācāra, the relevant analogue of *śruti* is Buddhist *āgama*, but its literal surface is governed by pedagogical intention (*abhiprāya*) [[Vv 8](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/8-abhiprayavasat)]. Vasubandhu argues that the Buddha's teaching of the existence of the sense-fields was delivered for disciples requiring that instruction, while its doctrinal import is re-read in terms of cognition and its own seeds rather than of external objects [[Vv 9](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/9-svabijad-vijnaptih)].

In Trika, the functional analogue of *śruti* is *āgama*: one revelation displayed by the all-seeing Lord and established across both the path of engagement and the path of withdrawal [[TĀ 35.23-24](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/35.23-24)]. Because that one Śāmbhava āgama addresses differentiated fruits and adhikāras, it can prescribe diverse upāyas without contradiction, and other śāstras are placed as graded articulations within that wider revelatory order [[TĀ 35.23-24](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/35.23-24)] [[TĀ 35.30](cite://abhinavagupta/tantraloka/35.30)].

The closest Jain analogue to *śruti* is *śruta-jñāna*, scriptural cognition [[TS 1.9-11](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-11)]. Umāsvāti counts *mati* and *śruta* among the five kinds of knowledge, classifies the first two as indirect (*parokṣa*), and defines *śruta* as arising on the basis of *mati* while ramifying into many divisions [[TS 1.9-11](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.9-11)] [[TS 1.20](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/1.20)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: Within this wave's acquisition rule, śruti is not a core Pāṇinian-Vaiyākaraṇa doctrinal term on the model of śabda, sphoṭa, or kāraka, and the on-disk grammatical primaries needed to argue otherwise are absent.

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'scripture' or 'revelation.' Both import Abrahamic baggage: a historically delivered message authored by God or a prophet and bounded as a book. *Śruti* instead names an authorless heard corpus plus the Mīmāṃsā-Vedānta theory that such language is intrinsically valid and hermeneutically ordered. Leaving the term untranslated, or glossing it once as 'the heard Veda,' usually preserves the metaphysics better.
